item.title: The “A”s Have It
item.description:
At least in the world of old school video game publishers

Introduction

The Atari 2600, or Video Computer System (VCS), introduced in 1977, would ultimately become the first commercially-successful video game console which accepted plug-in ROM cartridges, allowing the consumer to expand their library of games without having to buy a new console.¹

The system made inroads into the home video game market for its first couple of years despite a much higher price than its predecessors, thanks in large part to its removable game cartridges. However, the event that propelled the Atari 2600 into the stratosphere was the release of the Space Invaders® cartridge in 1980. Space Invaders, a huge arcade hit designed by Tomohiro Nishikado of the Japanese game company Taito, was the first significant license of an arcade megahit to the home video game market.

When Space Invaders arrived on the Atari, not only did the cartridge sell like crazy, but the Atari 2600 machine quadrupled in sales overnight as the crowd of enthusiasts needed the game console to play the cartridge. A niche electronics product was quickly becoming big business.

Opportunity

The meteoric growth of the Atari 2600 platform caught the eyes of many, including a team of top game developers at Atari, namely David Crane, Bob Whitehead, Alan Miller and Larry Kaplan.

It all started when Atari management made the ill-advised decision to internally publish a list of the sales of the top game cartridges for the previous year, apparently with the intent of educating the development team on what type of games were selling well. Of course, this goal could have been achieved without including actual dollar amounts of sales generated by game.

Predictably, each game developer focused on how their individual titles were doing. And, as it turns out, showing four guys who were making annual salaries of about $20,000 per year that their games had collectively generated $60 million in sales for the company didn’t go over very well.

When they challenged their boss on this inequity, requesting some sort of bonus or royalty structure for creative successes, they were quickly turned down, and told that they were no more important than the guy on the assembly line who screwed the cartridges together. In hindsight, that approach may have been a mistake.

Not surprisingly, the group of star designers spun off Atari and, in partnership with Jim Levy, a skilled marketing executive, co-founded their own company. For now, we’ll call them the Fab 5.

What should we name our new company?

In the Atari era in question, there was no E3 (Electronic Entertainment Expo), the humongous video game trade show. Rather, video games took up a small part of the giant Consumer Electronics Show, or CES, which ran twice a year, summer in Chicago and winter in Las Vegas. The pictures below, courtesy of The Verge feature article “Incredible photos from the CES vault: 1967 to 2014,”² show the Atari booth at CES 1979 on the left, and a small part of the show floor at CES 1980.

In the Jurassic era of the 1970’s, at the time when people weren’t carrying around a supercomputer in their pocket, if you wanted to find a company on the show floor you looked them up in the CES Directory. This was a thick, heavy paperback which contained an alphabetical listing of all of the exhibitors, including their floor location. When a CES attendee arrived to register and pick up their show badge, they were offered, and reluctantly accepted, a copy of the “show guide.” No one wanted to carry the stupid thing around, but, if you wanted to find a particular company, you had to have it. Honestly, without the guide, you couldn’t find anyone.

Given this reality, a legend arose around the Fab 5’s naming of their new venture. Since the new company would be having their coming out party at the winter CES (1980), in order to make sure that they would get foot traffic, they chose a name which alphabetically was listed earlier in the CES show guide than Atari. The theory was that anyone who was seeking out video game companies would immediately go to the CES guide and look up Atari, scanning from the beginning of the alphabetized list. Since Atari was pretty much¹ the only (video) game in town at the time, it was possible that once an attendee found Atari they would stop at the “A”s and be done with it. Ergo, if your company name came after Atari in the listing, you could be “hosed,” standing in an empty exhibit booth all day .

However, if your company name came before Atari alphabetically, at least you had a snowball’s chance in hell of being noticed.

As everyone knows, the Fab 5 chose the name Activision. ACtivision, of course, comes alphabetically before ATari. Could it be?

The Urban Legend is Born

Now industry insiders have always assumed that this is just an urban legend and bears no semblance to reality. The name Activision was just a great name for a video game company, as a clever combination of “active” and “television.” And in a multibillion-dollar industry, who’s going to make the critical decision of choosing a brand name over something so silly as alphabetical sorting.

Activision had a great run as the first 3rd party video game publisher starting in 1980. I joined the company in 1982. Unfortunately, in 1983, the Atari business crashed and burned (a story for another day, I assure you).

With the crash, the first “spinoff” at Activision was the loss of founders Bob Whitehead and Alan Miller. Discouraged by the direction of the company, and the drop in the AVSN stock price, Bob and Al left in 1985 to form a new company which would focus on computer software. Their first big hit was “Hardball,” designed by the talented Bob Whitehead.

Bob and Alan named their new company Accolade, Inc.

In 1986, Greg Fischbach, President of Activision International, left the company. Shortly thereafter, in 1987, Rob Holmes left the company to join Greg in co-founding a new video game company, along with Jimmy Scorpowski, an Activision sales rep.

They located the company in Glen Cove, New York. They called their new company Acclaim Entertainment.

In 1985, I, along with the rest of the Eastern Design Center, left Activision. We started as a contract game developer but quickly decide to form a new game publisher. In 1986, we published our first game on the Commodore 64, F-18 Hornet.

Absolute Entertainment was born.

Has the streak ended? Is it the end of an era?

It’s been many years since I co-founded Absolute Entertainment in 1986. The “A” streak had continued from 1979 to 1986. Not bad. For a long while, it looked like the streak had ended.

Or had it?

Out of nowhere, in 2019, Greg Fischbach, formerly of Activision and Acclaim Entertainment, stepped out of retirement to reenter the video game industry with a new publishing company.

He named it Accelerate Games.

What does the future hold?

Of course, the question remains, is the streak over? Have we reached the end of the “A” road of Atari/Activision alumni companies?

Or, put another way, in 2021, will anyone have the audacity to do it again?

[1] The Fairchild Channel F, released a year before the Atari 2600, also had swappable game cartridges, but the console never caught on.

[3] https://www.theverge.com/2013/1/4/3828848/ces-photo-history
